# UC-10.16.4 · Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) Trending

## Description

MTTR reflects containment and remediation speed once a detection exists. Sustained increases may signal playbook gaps, staffing shortages, or complex multi-domain incidents; decreases validate SOAR and runbook maturity.

## Value

MTTR reflects containment and remediation speed once a detection exists. Sustained increases may signal playbook gaps, staffing shortages, or complex multi-domain incidents; decreases validate SOAR and runbook maturity.

## Implementation

Map `closed_time` and open times from ES fields your deployment actually fills; use `transaction` or `stats` range if only event pairs exist. Exclude pending vendor response cases via a tag. Segment by `severity` or `security_domain` for actionable drilldowns. Align MTTR definitions with SLA contracts to avoid mismatched executive expectations.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security, Splunk SOAR (optional).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=notable` closed events, `sourcetype=stash` SOAR playbook duration summaries.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Map `closed_time` and open times from ES fields your deployment actually fills; use `transaction` or `stats` range if only event pairs exist. Exclude pending vendor response cases via a tag. Segment by `severity` or `security_domain` for actionable drilldowns. Align MTTR definitions with SLA contracts to avoid mismatched executive expectations.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=notable earliest=-180d status="Closed*"
| eval mttr_sec=coalesce(duration, if(isnotnull(closed_time), closed_time - _time, null()), time_to_close)
| where isnotnull(mttr_sec) AND mttr_sec>=0
| bin _time span=1mon
| stats median(mttr_sec) as median_mttr_sec p95(mttr_sec) as p95_mttr_sec count by _time
| eval median_mttr_hrs=round(median_mttr_sec/3600,2)
| timechart span=1mon first(median_mttr_hrs) as median_mttr_hrs first(p95_mttr_sec) as p95_mttr_sec
```

Understanding this SPL

**Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) Trending** — MTTR reflects containment and remediation speed once a detection exists. Sustained increases may signal playbook gaps, staffing shortages, or complex multi-domain incidents; decreases validate SOAR and runbook maturity.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=notable` closed events, `sourcetype=stash` SOAR playbook duration summaries. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security, Splunk SOAR (optional). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: notable.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=notable, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **mttr_sec** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(mttr_sec) AND mttr_sec>=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **median_mttr_hrs** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1mon** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.


Step 3 — Validate
For the same tickets as MTTD, compare “closed/resolved” times from **ITSM** to the last notable update so the MTTR field is defined the same in both systems.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (median and P95 MTTR), stacked bar (case volume by closure month), table (top rules by MTTR).


## SPL

```spl
index=notable earliest=-180d status="Closed*"
| eval mttr_sec=coalesce(duration, if(isnotnull(closed_time), closed_time - _time, null()), time_to_close)
| where isnotnull(mttr_sec) AND mttr_sec>=0
| bin _time span=1mon
| stats median(mttr_sec) as median_mttr_sec p95(mttr_sec) as p95_mttr_sec count by _time
| eval median_mttr_hrs=round(median_mttr_sec/3600,2)
| timechart span=1mon first(median_mttr_hrs) as median_mttr_hrs first(p95_mttr_sec) as p95_mttr_sec
```

## Visualization

Line chart (median and P95 MTTR), stacked bar (case volume by closure month), table (top rules by MTTR).
